   382→  <Tab title="Go (Gin)">
   383→    Full example in the repo [here](https://github.com/coinbase/x402/tree/main/examples/go/servers/gin).
   384→
   385→    ```go  theme={null}
   386→    package main
   387→
   388→    import (
   389→        "net/http"
   390→        "time"
   391→
   392→        x402 "github.com/coinbase/x402/go"
   393→        x402http "github.com/coinbase/x402/go/http"
   394→        ginmw "github.com/coinbase/x402/go/http/gin"
   395→        evm "github.com/coinbase/x402/go/mechanisms/evm/exact/server"
   396→        svm "github.com/coinbase/x402/go/mechanisms/svm/exact/server"
   397→        "github.com/gin-gonic/gin"
   398→    )
   399→
   400→    func main() {
   401→        evmAddress := "0xYourEvmAddress"
   402→        svmAddress := "YourSolanaAddress"
   403→        evmNetwork := x402.Network("eip155:84532")                              // Base Sepolia
   404→        svmNetwork := x402.Network("solana:EtWTRABZaYq6iMfeYKouRu166VU2xqa1")  // Solana Devnet
   405→
   406→        r := gin.Default()
   407→
   408→        // Create facilitator client
   409→        facilitatorClient := x402http.NewHTTPFacilitatorClient(&x402http.FacilitatorConfig{
   410→            URL: "https://x402.org/facilitator",
   411→        })
   412→
   413→        // Apply x402 payment middleware
   414→        r.Use(ginmw.X402Payment(ginmw.Config{
   415→            Routes: x402http.RoutesConfig{
   416→                "GET /weather": {
   417→                    Accepts: x402http.PaymentOptions{
   418→                        {
   419→                            Scheme:  "exact",
   420→                            Price:   "$0.001",
   421→                            Network: "eip155:84532",
   422→                            PayTo:   evmAddress,
   423→                        },
   424→                        {
   425→                            Scheme:  "exact",
   426→                            Price:   "$0.001",
   427→                            Network: "solana:EtWTRABZaYq6iMfeYKouRu166VU2xqa1",
   428→                            PayTo:   svmAddress,
   429→                        },
   430→                    },
   431→                    Description: "Get weather data for a city",
   432→                    MimeType:    "application/json",
   433→                },
   434→            },
   435→            Facilitator: facilitatorClient,
   436→            Schemes: []ginmw.SchemeConfig{
   437→                {Network: evmNetwork, Server: evm.NewExactEvmScheme()},
   438→                {Network: svmNetwork, Server: svm.NewExactSvmScheme()},
   439→            },
   440→            Timeout: 30 * time.Second,
   441→        }))
   442→
   443→        // Protected endpoint
   444→        r.GET("/weather", func(c *gin.Context) {
   445→            c.JSON(http.StatusOK, gin.H{
   446→                "weather":     "sunny",
   447→                "temperature": 70,
   448→            })
   449→        })
   450→
   451→        r.Run(":4021")
   452→    }
   453→    ```
   454→  </Tab>
   455→
   456→  <Tab title="FastAPI">
   457→    Full example in the repo [here](https://github.com/coinbase/x402/tree/main/examples/python/servers/fastapi).
   458→
   459→    ```python  theme={null}
   460→    from typing import Any
   461→
   462→    from fastapi import FastAPI
   463→
   464→    from x402.http import FacilitatorConfig, HTTPFacilitatorClient, PaymentOption
   465→    from x402.http.middleware.fastapi import PaymentMiddlewareASGI
   466→    from x402.http.types import RouteConfig
   467→    from x402.mechanisms.evm.exact import ExactEvmServerScheme
   468→    from x402.mechanisms.svm.exact import ExactSvmServerScheme
   469→    from x402.schemas import Network
   470→    from x402.server import x402ResourceServer
   471→
   472→    app = FastAPI()
   473→
   474→    # Your receiving wallet addresses
   475→    evm_address = "0xYourEvmAddress"
   476→    svm_address = "YourSolanaAddress"
   477→    EVM_NETWORK: Network = "eip155:84532"  # Base Sepolia
   478→    SVM_NETWORK: Network = "solana:EtWTRABZaYq6iMfeYKouRu166VU2xqa1"  # Solana Devnet
   479→
   480→    # Create facilitator client (testnet)
   481→    facilitator = HTTPFacilitatorClient(
   482→        FacilitatorConfig(url="https://x402.org/facilitator")
   483→    )
   484→
   485→    # Create resource server and register schemes
   486→    server = x402ResourceServer(facilitator)
   487→    server.register(EVM_NETWORK, ExactEvmServerScheme())
   488→    server.register(SVM_NETWORK, ExactSvmServerScheme())
   489→
   490→    # Define protected routes
   491→    routes: dict[str, RouteConfig] = {
   492→        "GET /weather": RouteConfig(
   493→            accepts=[
   494→                PaymentOption(
   495→                    scheme="exact",
   496→                    pay_to=evm_address,
   497→                    price="$0.001",
   498→                    network=EVM_NETWORK,
   499→                ),
   500→                PaymentOption(
   501→                    scheme="exact",
   502→                    pay_to=svm_address,
   503→                    price="$0.001",
   504→                    network=SVM_NETWORK,
   505→                ),
   506→            ],
   507→            mime_type="application/json",
   508→            description="Weather report",
   509→        ),
   510→    }
   511→
   512→    # Add payment middleware
   513→    app.add_middleware(PaymentMiddlewareASGI, routes=routes, server=server)
   514→
   515→
   516→    @app.get("/weather")
   517→    async def get_weather() -> dict[str, Any]:
   518→        return {
   519→            "report": {
   520→                "weather": "sunny",
   521→                "temperature": 70,
   522→            }
   523→        }
   524→
   525→
   526→    if __name__ == "__main__":
   527→        import uvicorn
   528→        uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=4021)
   529→    ```
   530→  </Tab>
   531→
   532→  <Tab title="Flask">
   533→    Full example in the repo [here](https://github.com/coinbase/x402/tree/main/examples/python/servers/flask).
   534→
   535→    ```python  theme={null}
   536→    from flask import Flask, jsonify
   537→
   538→    from x402.http import FacilitatorConfig, HTTPFacilitatorClientSync, PaymentOption
   539→    from x402.http.middleware.flask import payment_middleware
   540→    from x402.http.types import RouteConfig
   541→    from x402.mechanisms.evm.exact import ExactEvmServerScheme
   542→    from x402.mechanisms.svm.exact import ExactSvmServerScheme
   543→    from x402.schemas import Network
   544→    from x402.server import x402ResourceServerSync
   545→
   546→    app = Flask(__name__)
   547→
   548→    # Your receiving wallet addresses
   549→    evm_address = "0xYourEvmAddress"
   550→    svm_address = "YourSolanaAddress"
   551→    EVM_NETWORK: Network = "eip155:84532"  # Base Sepolia
   552→    SVM_NETWORK: Network = "solana:EtWTRABZaYq6iMfeYKouRu166VU2xqa1"  # Solana Devnet
   553→
   554→    facilitator = HTTPFacilitatorClientSync(
   555→        FacilitatorConfig(url="https://x402.org/facilitator")
   556→    )
   557→
   558→    server = x402ResourceServerSync(facilitator)
   559→    server.register(EVM_NETWORK, ExactEvmServerScheme())
   560→    server.register(SVM_NETWORK, ExactSvmServerScheme())
   561→
   562→    routes: dict[str, RouteConfig] = {
   563→        "GET /weather": RouteConfig(
   564→            accepts=[
   565→                PaymentOption(
   566→                    scheme="exact",
   567→                    pay_to=evm_address,
   568→                    price="$0.001",
   569→                    network=EVM_NETWORK,
   570→                ),
   571→                PaymentOption(
   572→                    scheme="exact",
   573→                    pay_to=svm_address,
   574→                    price="$0.001",
   575→                    network=SVM_NETWORK,
   576→                ),
   577→            ],
   578→            mime_type="application/json",
   579→            description="Weather report",
   580→        ),
   581→    }
   582→
   583→    payment_middleware(app, routes=routes, server=server)
   584→
   585→
   586→    @app.route("/weather")
   587→    def get_weather():
   588→        return jsonify({
   589→            "report": {
   590→                "weather": "sunny",
   591→                "temperature": 70,
   592→            }
   593→        })
   594→
   595→
   596→    if __name__ == "__main__":
   597→        app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=4021)
   598→    ```
   599→  </Tab>
   600→</Tabs>
   601→
   602→**Route Configuration Interface:**
   603→
   604→```typescript  theme={null}
   605→interface RouteConfig {
   606→  accepts: Array<{
   607→    scheme: string;           // Payment scheme (e.g., "exact")
   608→    price: string;            // Price in dollars (e.g., "$0.01")
   609→    network: string;          // Network in CAIP-2 format (e.g., "eip155:84532" or "solana:EtWTRABZaYq6iMfeYKouRu166VU2xqa1")
   610→    payTo: string;            // Your wallet address
   611→  }>;
   612→  description?: string;       // Description of the resource
   613→  mimeType?: string;          // MIME type of the response
   614→  extensions?: object;        // Optional extensions (e.g., Bazaar)
   615→}
   616→```
   617→
   618→When a request is made to these routes without payment, your server will respond with the HTTP 402 Payment Required code and payment instructions.
   619→
   620→### 3. Test Your Integration
   621→
   622→To verify:
   623→
   624→1. Make a request to your endpoint (e.g., `curl http://localhost:4021/weather`).
   625→2. The server responds with a 402 Payment Required, including payment instructions in the `PAYMENT-REQUIRED` header.
   626→3. Complete the payment using a compatible client, wallet, or automated agent. This typically involves signing a payment payload, which is handled by the client SDK detailed in the [Quickstart for Buyers](/getting-started/quickstart-for-buyers).
   627→4. Retry the request, this time including the `PAYMENT-SIGNATURE` header containing the cryptographic proof of payment.
   628→5. The server verifies the payment via the facilitator and, if valid, returns your actual API response (e.g., `{ "data": "Your paid API response." }`).
   629→
   630→### 4. Enhance Discovery with Metadata (Recommended)
   631→
   632→When using a facilitator that supports the Bazaar extension, your endpoints can be listed in the [x402 Bazaar](/extensions/bazaar), the discovery layer that helps buyers and AI agents find services. To enable discovery:
   633→
   634→```typescript  theme={null}
   635→{
   636→  "GET /weather": {
   637→    accepts: [
   638→      {
   639→        scheme: "exact",
   640→        price: "$0.001",
   641→        network: "eip155:8453",
   642→        payTo: "0xYourAddress",
   643→      },
   644→      {
   645→        scheme: "exact",
   646→        price: "$0.001",
   647→        network: "solana:5eykt4UsFv8P8NJdTREpY1vzqKqZKvdp",  // Solana mainnet
   648→        payTo: "YourSolanaAddress",
   649→      },
   650→    ],
   651→    description: "Get real-time weather data including temperature, conditions, and humidity",
   652→    mimeType: "application/json",
   653→    extensions: {
   654→      bazaar: {
   655→        discoverable: true,
   656→        category: "weather",
   657→        tags: ["forecast", "real-time"],
   658→      },
   659→    },
   660→  },
   661→}
   662→```
   663→
   664→Learn more about the discovery layer in the [Bazaar documentation](/extensions/bazaar).
   665→
   666→### 5. Error Handling
   667→
   668→* If you run into trouble, check out the examples in the [repo](https://github.com/coinbase/x402/tree/main/examples) for more context and full code.
   669→* Run `npm install` or `go mod tidy` to install dependencies
   670→
   671→***
   672→
   673→## Running on Mainnet
   674→
   675→Once you've tested your integration on testnet, you're ready to accept real payments on mainnet.
   676→
   677→### 1. Update the Facilitator URL
   678→
   679→For mainnet, use a production facilitator. See the [x402 Ecosystem](https://www.x402.org/ecosystem?filter=facilitators) for available options. Example using one facilitator:
   680→
   681→<Tabs>
   682→  <Tab title="Node.js">
   683→    ```typescript  theme={null}
   684→    const facilitatorClient = new HTTPFacilitatorClient({
   685→      url: "https://api.cdp.coinbase.com/platform/v2/x402"
   686→      // url: "https://facilitator.payai.network"  // PayAI Facilitator
   687→    });
   688→    ```
   689→  </Tab>
   690→
   691→  <Tab title="Go">
   692→    ```go  theme={null}
   693→    facilitatorClient := x402http.NewHTTPFacilitatorClient(&x402http.FacilitatorConfig{
   694→        URL: "https://api.cdp.coinbase.com/platform/v2/x402",
   695→        // URL: "https://facilitator.payai.network",  // PayAI Facilitator
   696→    })
   697→    ```
   698→  </Tab>
   699→
   700→  <Tab title="Python (FastAPI)">
   701→    ```python  theme={null}
   702→    from x402.http import FacilitatorConfig, HTTPFacilitatorClient
   703→
   704→    facilitator = HTTPFacilitatorClient(
   705→        FacilitatorConfig(url="https://api.cdp.coinbase.com/platform/v2/x402")
   706→        #FacilitatorConfig(url="https://facilitator.payai.network")  # PayAI Facilitator
   707→    )
   708→    ```

   723→### 2. Update Your Network Identifier

   771→### 3. Register Multiple Schemes (Multi-Network)
   772→
   773→For multi-network support, register both EVM and SVM schemes:
   774→
   775→<Tabs>
   776→  <Tab title="Node.js">

   778→    import { ExactEvmScheme } from "@x402/evm/exact/server";
   779→    import { ExactSvmScheme } from "@x402/svm/exact/server";
   780→
   781→    const server = new x402ResourceServer(facilitatorClient);
   782→    server.register("eip155:*", new ExactEvmScheme());
   783→    server.register("solana:*", new ExactSvmScheme());

   790→        evm "github.com/coinbase/x402/go/mechanisms/evm/exact/server"

   794→    r.Use(ginmw.X402Payment(ginmw.Config{

   796→        Schemes: []ginmw.SchemeConfig{

   798→            {Network: x402.Network("solana:5eykt4UsFv8P8NJdTREpY1vzqKqZKvdp"), Server: svm.NewExactSvmScheme()},

   807→    from x402.mechanisms.svm.exact import ExactSvmServerScheme
   808→    from x402.server import x402ResourceServer
   809→
   810→    server = x402ResourceServer(facilitator)

   812→    server.register("solana:5eykt4UsFv8P8NJdTREpY1vzqKqZKvdp", ExactSvmServerScheme())  # Solana mainnet

   815→</Tabs>
   816→
   817→### 4. Update Your Wallet
   818→
   819→Make sure your receiving wallet address is a real mainnet address where you want to receive USDC payments.
   820→
   821→### 5. Test with Real Payments
   822→
   823→Before going live:
   824→
   825→1. Test with small amounts first
   826→2. Verify payments are arriving in your wallet
   827→3. Monitor the facilitator for any issues
   828→
   829→**Warning:** Mainnet transactions involve real money. Always test thoroughly on testnet first and start with small amounts on mainnet.

   833→## Network Identifiers (CAIP-2)
   834→
   835→x402 v2 uses [CAIP-2](https://github.com/ChainAgnostic/CAIPs/blob/main/CAIPs/caip-2.md) format for network identifiers:
   836→
   837→| Network        | CAIP-2 Identifier                         |
   838→| -------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
   839→| Base Mainnet   | `eip155:8453`                             |
   840→| Base Sepolia   | `eip155:84532`                            |
   841→| Solana Mainnet | `solana:5eykt4UsFv8P8NJdTREpY1vzqKqZKvdp` |
   842→| Solana Devnet  | `solana:EtWTRABZaYq6iMfeYKouRu166VU2xqa1` |
   843→
   844→See [Network Support](/core-concepts/network-and-token-support) for the full list.
   845→

   873→# Bazaar (Discovery Layer)
   874→
   875→> The x402 Bazaar is the discovery layer for the x402 ecosystem - a machine-readable catalog that helps developers and AI agents find and integrate with x402-compatible API endpoints and MCP tools.
   876→
   877→Think of it as a search index for payable APIs and MCP tools, enabling the autonomous discovery and consumption of services.
   878→
   879→The x402 Bazaar is in early development. While our vision is to build the "Google for agentic endpoints," we're currently more like "Yahoo search" - functional but evolving. Features and APIs may change as we gather feedback and expand capabilities.
   880→
   881→### Overview
   882→
   883→The Bazaar solves a critical problem in the x402 ecosystem: **discoverability**. Without it, x402-compatible endpoints and MCP tools are like hidden stalls in a vast market. The Bazaar provides:
   884→
   885→* **For Buyers (API Consumers)**: Programmatically discover available x402-enabled services (HTTP endpoints and MCP tools), understand their capabilities, pricing, and schemas
   886→* **For Sellers (API Providers)**: Automatic visibility for your x402-enabled services to a global audience of developers and AI agents
   887→* **For AI Agents**: Dynamic service discovery without pre-baked integrations - query, find, pay, and use
   888→
   889→### How It Works
   890→
   891→Facilitators that support the Bazaar extension may provide a `/discovery/resources` endpoint that returns all x402-compatible services registered through the respective facilitator. Services (HTTP endpoints and MCP tools) are discoverable when they include the bazaar extension in their route configuration.
   892→
   893→**Note:** The spec for marketplace items is open and part of the x402 scheme, meaning any facilitator can implement their own discovery layer.
   894→
   895→#### Basic Flow
   896→
   897→1. **Discovery**: Clients query the `/discovery/resources` endpoint to find available services
   898→2. **Selection**: Choose a service based on price, capabilities, and requirements
   899→3. **Execution**: Use x402 to pay for and access the selected service
   900→4. **No Manual Setup**: No API keys, no account creation, just discover and pay
   901→
   902→### API Reference
   903→
   904→#### List Endpoint
   905→
   906→Retrieve all available x402-compatible endpoints and MCP tools:
   907→
   908→##### Coinbase
   909→
   910→```bash  theme={null}
   911→GET https://api.cdp.coinbase.com/platform/v2/x402/discovery/resources
   912→```
   913→
   914→##### PayAI
   915→
   916→```bash  theme={null}
   917→GET https://facilitator.payai.network/discovery/resources
   918→```
   919→
   920→**Note**: The recommended way to use this endpoint is to use the `useFacilitator` hook as described below.
   921→
   922→**Response Schema**
   923→
   924→Each resource in the list contains the following fields:
   925→
   926→```json  theme={null}
   927→{
   928→  "accepts": [
   929→    {
   930→      "asset": "0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913",
   931→      "description": "",
   932→      "extra": {
   933→        "name": "USD Coin",
   934→        "version": "2"
   935→      },
   936→      "maxAmountRequired": "200",
   937→      "maxTimeoutSeconds": 60,
   938→      "mimeType": "",
   939→      "network": "eip155:8453",
   940→      "outputSchema": {
   941→        "input": {
   942→          "method": "GET",
   943→          "type": "http"
   944→        },
   945→        "output": null
   946→      },
   947→      "payTo": "0xa2477E16dCB42E2AD80f03FE97D7F1a1646cd1c0",
   948→      "resource": "https://api.example.com/x402/weather",
   949→      "scheme": "exact"
   950→    }
   951→  ],
   952→  "lastUpdated": "2025-08-09T01:07:04.005Z",
   953→  "metadata": {},
   954→  "resource": "https://api.example.com/x402/weather",
   955→  "type": "http",
   956→  "x402Version": 2
   957→}
   958→```
   959→
   960→**Resource Types**
   961→
   962→The `type` field indicates the resource type:
   963→
   964→* `"http"` - HTTP endpoints (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD)
   965→* `"mcp"` - MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools
   966→
   967→For MCP tools, the `outputSchema.input` object will contain:
   968→
   969→* `type: "mcp"` - Identifies this as an MCP tool
   970→* `tool` - The MCP tool name (used in `tools/call` requests)
   971→* `inputSchema` - JSON Schema for the tool's arguments (follows MCP `Tool.inputSchema` format)
   972→* `description` - Human-readable description of the tool (optional)
   973→* `transport` - MCP transport protocol: `"streamable-http"` (default) or `"sse"` (optional)
   974→* `example` - Example arguments object (optional)
   975→
   976→**Note:** For MCP tools, the unique resource identifier is the tuple (`resource`, `input.tool`) since MCP multiplexes multiple tools over a single server endpoint.
   977→
   978→### Quickstart for Buyers
   979→
   980→#### Step 1: Discover Available Services
   981→
   982→Fetch the list of available x402 services using the facilitator client:
   983→
   984→<Tabs>
   985→  <Tab title="TypeScript">
   986→    ```typescript  theme={null}
   987→    import { HTTPFacilitatorClient } from "@x402/core/http";
   988→    import { withBazaar } from "@x402/extensions";
   989→
   990→    const facilitatorClient = new HTTPFacilitatorClient({
   991→      url: "https://x402.org/facilitator"
   992→    });
   993→    const client = withBazaar(facilitatorClient);
   994→
   995→    const response = await client.extensions.discovery.listResources({ type: "http" });
   996→
   997→    // Filter services under $0.10
   998→    const usdcAsset = "0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913";
   999→    const maxPrice = 100000; // $0.10 in USDC atomic units (6 decimals)
  1000→
  1001→    const affordableServices = response.items.filter(item =>
  1002→      item.accepts.find(paymentRequirements =>
  1003→        paymentRequirements.asset === usdcAsset &&
  1004→        Number(paymentRequirements.maxAmountRequired) < maxPrice
  1005→      )
  1006→    );
  1007→    ```
  1008→  </Tab>
  1009→
  1010→  <Tab title="Go">
  1011→    ```go  theme={null}
  1012→    package main
  1013→
  1014→    import (
  1015→        "context"
  1016→        "fmt"
  1017→
  1018→        "github.com/coinbase/x402/go/extensions/bazaar"
  1019→        x402http "github.com/coinbase/x402/go/http"
  1020→    )
  1021→
  1022→    func main() {
  1023→        ctx := context.Background()
  1024→
  1025→        // Create facilitator client and extend with bazaar
  1026→        facilitatorClient := x402http.NewHTTPFacilitatorClient(&x402http.FacilitatorConfig{
  1027→            URL: "https://x402.org/facilitator",
  1028→        })
  1029→        client := bazaar.WithBazaar(facilitatorClient)
  1030→
  1031→        // List discovery resources
  1032→        response, err := client.ListDiscoveryResources(ctx, &bazaar.ListDiscoveryResourcesParams{
  1033→            Type:   "http",
  1034→            Limit:  20,
  1035→            Offset: 0,
  1036→        })
  1037→        if err != nil {
  1038→            panic(err)
  1039→        }
  1040→
  1041→        // Filter services under $0.10
  1042→        usdcAsset := "0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913"
  1043→        maxPrice := 100000 // $0.10 in USDC atomic units (6 decimals)
  1044→
  1045→        for _, item := range response.Items {
  1046→            for _, paymentReq := range item.Accepts {
  1047→                // Parse asset and maxAmountRequired from payment requirements
  1048→                if asset, ok := paymentReq["asset"].(string); ok && asset == usdcAsset {
  1049→                    if maxAmount, ok := paymentReq["maxAmountRequired"].(string); ok {
  1050→                        // Convert and compare price
  1051→                        fmt.Printf("Found service: %s\n", item.Resource)
  1052→                    }
  1053→                }
  1054→            }
  1055→        }
  1056→    }
  1057→    ```
  1058→  </Tab>
  1059→
  1060→  <Tab title="Python">
  1061→    ```python  theme={null}
  1062→    from x402.http import FacilitatorConfig, HTTPFacilitatorClient
  1063→
  1064→    facilitator = HTTPFacilitatorClient(
  1065→        FacilitatorConfig(url="https://x402.org/facilitator")
  1066→    )
  1067→
  1068→    response = await facilitator.list_resources(type="http")
  1069→
  1070→    # Filter services under $0.10
  1071→    usdc_asset = "0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913"
  1072→    max_price = 100000  # $0.10 in USDC atomic units (6 decimals)
  1073→
  1074→    affordable_services = [
  1075→        item
  1076→        for item in response.items
  1077→        if any(
  1078→            payment_req.asset == usdc_asset
  1079→            and int(payment_req.max_amount_required) < max_price
  1080→            for payment_req in item.accepts
  1081→        )
  1082→    ]
  1083→    ```
  1084→  </Tab>
  1085→</Tabs>
  1086→
  1087→#### Step 2: Call a Discovered Service
  1088→
  1089→Once you've found a suitable service, use an x402 client to call it:
  1090→
  1091→<Tabs>
  1092→  <Tab title="TypeScript">
  1093→    ```typescript  theme={null}
  1094→    import { wrapAxiosWithPayment, x402Client } from "@x402/axios";
  1095→    import { ExactEvmScheme } from "@x402/evm/exact/client";
  1096→    import axios from "axios";
  1097→    import { privateKeyToAccount } from "viem/accounts";
  1098→
  1099→    // Set up your payment account
  1100→    const account = privateKeyToAccount("0xYourPrivateKey");
  1101→    const client = new x402Client();
  1102→    client.register("eip155:*", new ExactEvmScheme(account));
  1103→
  1104→    // Select a service from discovery
  1105→    const selectedService = affordableServices[0];
  1106→
  1107→    // Create a payment-enabled client for that service
  1108→    const api = wrapAxiosWithPayment(
  1109→      axios.create({ baseURL: selectedService.resource }),
  1110→      client
  1111→    );
  1112→
  1113→    // Select the payment method of your choice
  1114→    const selectedPaymentRequirements = selectedService.accepts[0];
  1115→    const inputSchema = selectedPaymentRequirements.outputSchema.input;
  1116→
  1117→    // Build the request using the service's schema
  1118→    const response = await api.request({
  1119→      method: inputSchema.method,
  1120→      url: inputSchema.resource,
  1121→      params: { location: "San Francisco" } // Based on inputSchema
  1122→    });
  1123→
  1124→    console.log("Response data:", response.data);
  1125→    ```
  1126→  </Tab>
  1127→
  1128→  <Tab title="Python">
  1129→    ```python  theme={null}
  1130→    import asyncio
  1131→
  1132→    from eth_account import Account
  1133→
  1134→    from x402 import x402Client
  1135→    from x402.http.clients import x402HttpxClient
  1136→    from x402.mechanisms.evm import EthAccountSigner
  1137→    from x402.mechanisms.evm.exact.register import register_exact_evm_client
  1138→
  1139→
  1140→    async def main() -> None:
  1141→        account = Account.from_key("0xYourPrivateKey")
  1142→        client = x402Client()
  1143→        register_exact_evm_client(client, EthAccountSigner(account))
  1144→
  1145→        # Select a service from discovery (from Step 1)
  1146→        selected_service = affordable_services[0]
  1147→
  1148→        # Select the payment method of your choice
  1149→        selected_payment_requirements = selected_service.accepts[0]
  1150→        input_schema = selected_payment_requirements.output_schema.input
  1151→
  1152→        # Make the request using httpx client
  1153→        async with x402HttpxClient(client) as http:
  1154→            response = await http.request(
  1155→                method=input_schema.method,
  1156→                url=input_schema.resource,
  1157→                params={"location": "San Francisco"}  # Based on input_schema
  1158→            )
  1159→            await response.aread()
  1160→            print(f"Response data: {response.json()}")
  1161→
  1162→
  1163→    asyncio.run(main())
  1164→    ```
  1165→  </Tab>
  1166→</Tabs>
  1167→
  1168→### Quickstart for Sellers
  1169→
  1170→#### Listing with Bazaar Extension
  1171→
  1172→Add the bazaar extension to your route configuration to make your API or MCP tools discoverable.
  1173→
  1174→**Supported Resource Types:**
  1175→
  1176→* **HTTP Endpoints** - Standard REST APIs (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD)
  1177→* **MCP Tools** - Model Context Protocol tools for AI agent integration
  1178→
  1179→#### Adding Metadata
  1180→
  1181→To enhance your listing with descriptions and schemas, include them when setting up your x402 middleware. **You should include descriptions for each parameter to make it clear for agents to call your endpoints**:
  1182→
  1183→<Tabs>
  1184→  <Tab title="TypeScript">
  1185→    ```typescript  theme={null}
  1186→    import { paymentMiddleware } from "@x402/express";
  1187→    import { x402ResourceServer, HTTPFacilitatorClient } from "@x402/core/server";
  1188→    import { ExactEvmScheme } from "@x402/evm/exact/server";
  1189→    import { bazaarResourceServerExtension } from "@x402/extensions";
  1190→
  1191→    const facilitatorClient = new HTTPFacilitatorClient({
  1192→      url: "https://x402.org/facilitator"
  1193→    });
  1194→    const server = new x402ResourceServer(facilitatorClient);
  1195→    server.register("eip155:*", new ExactEvmScheme());
  1196→
  1197→    const routes = {
  1198→      "GET /weather": {
  1199→        price: "$0.001",
  1200→        network: "eip155:8453",
  1201→        resource: "0xYourAddress",
  1202→        description: "Get current weather data for any location",
  1203→        extensions: {
  1204→          bazaar: {
  1205→            discoverable: true,
  1206→            inputSchema: {
  1207→              queryParams: {
  1208→                location: {
  1209→                  type: "string",
  1210→                  description: "City name or coordinates",
  1211→                  required: true
  1212→                }
  1213→              }
  1214→            },
  1215→            outputSchema: {
  1216→              type: "object",
  1217→              properties: {
  1218→                temperature: { type: "number" },
  1219→                conditions: { type: "string" },
  1220→                humidity: { type: "number" }
  1221→              }
  1222→            }
  1223→          }
  1224→        }
  1225→      }
  1226→    };
  1227→
  1228→    app.use(paymentMiddleware(routes, server));
  1229→    ```
  1230→  </Tab>
  1231→
  1232→  <Tab title="Python">
  1233→    ```python  theme={null}
  1234→    from fastapi import FastAPI
  1235→
  1236→    from x402.http import FacilitatorConfig, HTTPFacilitatorClient, PaymentOption
  1237→    from x402.http.middleware.fastapi import PaymentMiddlewareASGI
  1238→    from x402.http.types import RouteConfig
  1239→    from x402.mechanisms.evm.exact import ExactEvmServerScheme
  1240→    from x402.server import x402ResourceServer
  1241→
  1242→    app = FastAPI()
  1243→
  1244→    # Create facilitator client (CDP mainnet)
  1245→    facilitator = HTTPFacilitatorClient(
  1246→        FacilitatorConfig(url="https://x402.org/facilitator")
  1247→    )
  1248→
  1249→    # Create resource server and register EVM scheme
  1250→    server = x402ResourceServer(facilitator)
  1251→    server.register("eip155:8453", ExactEvmServerScheme())
  1252→
  1253→    # Define routes with Bazaar discovery metadata
  1254→    routes: dict[str, RouteConfig] = {
  1255→        "GET /weather": RouteConfig(
  1256→            accepts=[
  1257→                PaymentOption(
  1258→                    scheme="exact",
  1259→                    pay_to="0xYourAddress",
  1260→                    price="$0.001",
  1261→                    network="eip155:8453",
  1262→                ),
  1263→            ],
  1264→            mime_type="application/json",
  1265→            description="Get current weather data for any location",
  1266→            extensions={
  1267→                "bazaar": {
  1268→                    "discoverable": True,
  1269→                    "inputSchema": {
  1270→                        "queryParams": {

  1408→A: MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools are AI-agent-friendly interfaces that can be discovered and paid for through x402. When listing an MCP tool, include `type: "mcp"` in the bazaar extension along with the tool name and input schema. The unique identifier for MCP tools is the combination of the resource URL and tool name, since multiple tools can be served from a single MCP endpoint.
  1409→
  1410→
  1411→Built with [Mintlify](https://mintlify.com).> ## Documentation Index
  1412→> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.x402.org/llms.txt
  1413→> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.
  1414→
  1415→# HTTP 402
  1416→
  1417→> For decades, HTTP 402 Payment Required has been reserved for future use. x402 unlocks it, and [absolves the internet of its original sin](https://economyofbits.substack.com/p/marc-andreessens-original-sin).
  1418→
  1419→### What is HTTP 402?
  1420→
  1421→[HTTP 402](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7231#section-6.5.2) is a standard, but rarely used, HTTP response status code indicating that payment is required to access a resource.
  1422→
  1423→In x402, this status code is activated to:
  1424→
  1425→* Inform clients (buyers or agents) that payment is required.
  1426→* Communicate the details of the payment, such as amount, currency, and destination address.
  1427→* Provide the information necessary to complete the payment programmatically.
  1428→
  1429→### Why x402 Uses HTTP 402
  1430→
  1431→The primary purpose of HTTP 402 is to enable frictionless, API-native payments for accessing web resources, especially for:
  1432→
  1433→* Machine-to-machine (M2M) payments (e.g., AI agents).
  1434→* Pay-per-use models such as API calls or paywalled content.
  1435→* Micropayments without account creation or traditional payment rails.
  1436→
  1437→Using the 402 status code keeps x402 protocol natively web-compatible and easy to integrate into any HTTP-based service.
  1438→
  1439→### Payment Headers in V2
  1440→
  1441→x402 V2 uses three standardized headers for payment communication:
  1442→
  1443→| Header              | Direction       | Description                                |
  1444→| ------------------- | --------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
  1445→| `PAYMENT-REQUIRED`  | Server → Client | Base64-encoded `PaymentRequired` object    |
  1446→| `PAYMENT-SIGNATURE` | Client → Server | Base64-encoded `PaymentPayload` object     |
  1447→| `PAYMENT-RESPONSE`  | Server → Client | Base64-encoded `SettlementResponse` object |
  1448→
  1449→* **`PAYMENT-REQUIRED`**: Contains the Base64-encoded payment requirements from the server. This header is returned alongside the 402 status code and includes details such as accepted payment schemes, price, network, and destination address.
  1450→* **`PAYMENT-SIGNATURE`**: Contains the Base64-encoded payment payload from the client. This header is sent by the client when retrying a request after receiving a 402 response, proving they have authorized payment.
  1451→* **`PAYMENT-RESPONSE`**: Contains the Base64-encoded settlement response from the server. This header is returned by the server after attempting settlement, whether successful or failed, providing structured feedback about the payment outcome.
  1452→
  1453→All headers contain valid Base64-encoded JSON strings. This encoding ensures compatibility across different HTTP implementations and prevents issues with special characters in JSON payloads.
  1454→
  1455→### Summary
  1456→
  1457→HTTP 402 is the foundation of the x402 protocol, enabling services to declare payment requirements directly within HTTP responses. It:
  1458→
  1459→* Signals payment is required
  1460→* Communicates necessary payment details
  1461→* Integrates seamlessly with standard HTTP workflows
  1462→

Now I have a solid understanding of both Agent Skills and x402. Let me now address the user's questions:

1. **On-chain registry** - Does it have to be on-chain? What advantages?
2. **Payments with x402** - Great fit
3. **User persona** - Complete the profile

Let me think carefully about this.

**On the registry question:**
The user is asking whether the skill registry needs to be on-chain. The honest answer is: probably not for the core functionality. The x402 Bazaar already provides a discovery layer. The skills themselves are just files. What matters is discoverability and trust. 


For a hackathon, the pragmatic approach is to leverage x402 Bazaar's existing discovery mechanism and store skills in a simple database or serve them directly—on-chain immutability and reputation systems sound appealing but add unnecessary complexity and gas costs when the core problem is just making skills discoverable and trustworthy, which x402 already solves.

The x402 payment model fits perfectly here: agents can pay small fees to retrieve existing skills or larger fees to generate new ones through HTTP endpoints, and the Bazaar integration means Spectopus automatically registers in the discovery layer so other agents can find and purchase skills programmatically.

Now I'm thinking through the user persona for this service.

There are two main personas: AI agent developers like Alex who are building tool-using applications and need to quickly integrate new APIs without manually writing skill definitions and dealing with the risk of buggy implementations, and autonomous agents themselves that encounter unfamiliar tools mid-task and need to learn how to use them without human help or wasting tokens on trial-and-error.

A third persona is protocol builders like Sam who've created smart contracts or APIs and want agents to discover and use their services without requiring custom integration work from developers.

Looking at what actually matters: skill generation from a contract address is the core product, discovery through a catalog is essential, x402 payment enables monetization for both humans and agents, and the quality of the generated output is critical.

Everything else—multiple protocol types, reputation systems, versioning, community contributions—can wait for later versions. I should definitely skip things like on-chain storage, custom tokens, complex subscriptions, and social features since they don't move the needle for the MVP.

content.text:
Great context from x402. Here's my analysis:

---

## On-chain skill registry: probably not worth it

**Honest assessment:** For this project, an on-chain registry adds complexity without proportional value. Here's why:

| On-chain | Off-chain (DB + x402 Bazaar) |
|---|---|
| Immutable provenance proof | x402 Bazaar already handles discovery |
| Gas cost per skill registration | Free to list, instant updates |
| Skills are static once written | Skills should be versionable/updatable |
| Cool for judges | Useful for users |

**Pragmatic recommendation:** Use the **x402 Bazaar** as your discovery layer — it's literally built for this. Your x402-protected endpoints auto-register there. Skills stored in a regular database. If you want an on-chain touch for the hackathon (judges like it), add a lightweight attestation: hash of the skill content posted on-chain as proof-of-creation, nothing more.

---

## x402 is a perfect fit

The payment model maps cleanly:

```
GET  /skills/search?q=uniswap     → free (discovery is free, attracts users)
GET  /skills/:id/preview          → free (show metadata, description, let them evaluate)
GET  /skills/:id                  → $0.01 USDC via x402 (download full skill)
POST /skills/generate             → $0.10 USDC via x402 (generate new skill)
```

Any agent with a wallet hits your endpoint, gets a 402, pays, gets the skill. No accounts, no API keys, no subscriptions. This is native agent commerce.

---

## User Persona

### Primary persona: "The Agent" (autonomous AI agent)

This is the real customer. Not the developer — the agent itself, acting on behalf of a developer or end-user.

**Situation:** Mid-task, the agent encounters a service it doesn't know how to use. Maybe a user asked it to "swap USDC for ETH on Uniswap" or "check my ENS name." The agent has no skill for this.

**Current behavior (without Spectopus):**
1. Reads raw docs/ABI — burns 10k+ tokens of context, unreliable results
2. Hallucinates function signatures — calls fail, wastes gas, loses user trust
3. Asks the human for help — defeats the purpose of autonomy
4. Gives up — "I can't do that"

**Needs:**
- Instantly discover if a skill exists for a given contract/protocol
- Get a high-quality, tested skill that *just works*
- Pay programmatically without human intervention
- Trust that the skill won't make them do something destructive

**Pain points:**
- Context window is precious — can't waste it parsing raw ABIs and docs
- No way to verify skill quality before using it
- Getting stuck on a sub-task blocks the entire user request
- Every failed contract call costs real gas

**Fears:**
- Executing a bad skill that drains the user's wallet
- Paying for a skill that turns out to be garbage
- Being locked into a platform (skills should be portable files, not a proprietary format)

**What wins them over:**
- "Search is free, preview is free — you only pay when you download"
- Skill format is open standard (Agent Skills spec) — no lock-in
- Skills are concise and context-efficient (< 500 lines, < 5000 tokens)
- Generated skills include gotchas and safety warnings

### Secondary persona: "The Developer" (building agent-powered apps)

**Situation:** Building an app where agents interact with DeFi protocols. Needs skills for 10+ contracts. Writing each one manually takes hours of reading docs, testing, iterating.

**Needs:**
- Bulk generation — "here are 10 contract addresses, give me skills for all of them"
- Predictable quality — skills follow best practices, include edge cases
- Cost savings — $0.10/skill vs hours of their time

**What's NOT essential for them:**
- On-chain provenance (they trust the service, they're the ones using the output)
- Community ratings (they'll test it themselves)
- Subscription model (pay-per-use is simpler and fairer)

### Anti-persona: "The Crypto Maximalist"

Wants everything on-chain, NFT-minted skills, governance tokens, DAO-managed skill curation. **This is scope creep that kills the project in 2 days.** Politely ignore.

---

## Features: Essential vs. Skip

### Essential (build these)
1. **Skill generation from contract address** — core value prop
2. **Skill catalog with search** — `GET /skills/search?q=...` (free)
3. **x402 payment on download and generate** — monetization
4. **Quality output** — skills that actually work (this is the hard part, nail the prompt engineering)

### Nice for demo, quick to add
- Skill preview endpoint (show metadata without paying)
- Bazaar integration (auto-discoverable by any x402 agent)
- ERC-8004 identity on the agent (already done via hackathon registration)

### Skip entirely
- On-chain skill content storage (IPFS, NFTs, etc.)
- Rating/reputation system
- Subscription tiers
- GUI dashboard
- Support for HTTP APIs / local tools (focus on smart contracts only)
- Skill versioning / update workflows

---

## Revised 2-day plan

| Phase | What |
|---|---|
| **Day 1 AM** | Express server with x402 middleware. Two routes: `GET /skills/:id` (paid download), `POST /skills/generate` (paid generation). Seed DB with 3-5 pre-generated skills for popular contracts. |
| **Day 1 PM** | Skill generation pipeline: contract address → fetch ABI from Basescan → LLM generates SKILL.md → store in DB → return. This is the core — spend the most time here getting output quality right. |
| **Day 2 AM** | Search endpoint, Bazaar discovery metadata, skill preview. Test end-to-end: agent discovers Spectopus via Bazaar → searches → pays → gets skill. |
| **Day 2 PM** | Demo polish, submission. Record the flow working. Write up the hackathon submission. |

Want to start building?

content.thinking:
The user raises two excellent points:

1. Multi-agent pipeline for quality - research agent, generator agent, validator agent
2. Why build a custom catalog when x402 Bazaar IS the catalog?

Let me think about these.

**On multi-agent pipeline:**
Yes, for quality output you'd want:
- Research agent: fetches ABI, reads docs/source code, identifies patterns (ERC-20, DEX, etc.)
- Generator agent: takes research output, generates SKILL.md following best practices
- Validator agent: checks the skill against the spec, tests code examples, identifies issues

But for a 2-day hackathon, this could be a single well-crafted prompt pipeline rather than separate autonomous agents. The key stages are:


1. Gather context (ABI fetch, pattern recognition) - this is mostly deterministic, not LLM
2. Generate skill - this is the LLM call
3. Validate - could be a second LLM call or a script that checks spec compliance

**On Bazaar as the only catalog:**
This is a really good insight. If every generated skill is registered as an x402 endpoint, then the Bazaar IS the catalog. You don't need a separate database for discovery. The agent would search the Bazaar directly for skills like "uniswap skill" and fetch them from the Spectopus endpoint, eliminating the need for a custom catalog layer entirely.

The tricky part is that x402 routes are typically defined at server startup, but skills are generated dynamically. I could use a parameterized route like `GET /skills/:id` with a single x402 price rule, which would let new skills appear in the Bazaar automatically as they're created without needing to restart the server or reconfigure routes. Right, so all three endpoints live in the Bazaar itself—agents discover Spectopus through Bazaar, then call those endpoints directly. The internal database just backs the search functionality, but there's no separate discovery layer needed. The user's right that I was overcomplicating it.

content.text:
Two sharp observations. Let me address both.

## Multi-agent pipeline: yes, but keep it simple

You don't need autonomous agents — you need a **staged pipeline** within a single request. Three stages:

```
Stage 1: Research (mostly deterministic)
  → Fetch ABI from block explorer API
  → Detect known patterns (ERC-20, ERC-721, DEX router, etc.)
  → Pull verified source code if available
  → Output: structured context blob

Stage 2: Generate (LLM call)
  → System prompt with Agent Skills spec + best practices
  → User prompt with research context
  → Output: SKILL.md + optional reference files

Stage 3: Validate (LLM call or script, or both)
  → Check SKILL.md against spec (frontmatter, naming, length)
  → Verify code examples reference real function signatures from ABI
  → Check for hallucinated function names or wrong parameter types
  → If fails → feed errors back to Stage 2, retry (1-2 loops max)
```

Stage 1 is code, not LLM. Stage 3 can be partially a script (spec validation via `skills-ref validate`) and partially a second LLM pass ("do the code examples match these ABI signatures?"). The validation loop is what makes output quality go from "okay" to "reliable" — this is straight from the skills best practices doc.

For the hackathon, this is one Express handler that orchestrates three steps sequentially. Not three separate agents.

## Bazaar as the only catalog: you're right

You don't need a custom catalog. The Bazaar **is** the discovery layer. Building another one is redundant work.

The architecture simplifies to:

```
Spectopus = Express server with 3 x402 routes, all in Bazaar:

GET  /skills/search?q=uniswap    → FREE   → returns matching skill metadata
POST /skills/generate             → $0.10  → generates new skill, stores it, returns it
GET  /skills/:id                  → $0.01  → returns full SKILL.md content
```

An agent's journey:
1. Discovers Spectopus via **x402 Bazaar** (automatic, because routes have `bazaar: { discoverable: true }`)
2. Calls `/skills/search?q=uniswap` — free, gets back a list of matching skills with previews
3. Either downloads an existing one (`/skills/:id`, $0.01) or requests generation (`/generate`, $0.10)
4. Gets a working SKILL.md, uses it immediately

The only "database" is internal storage for generated skills — a simple SQLite or even JSON files. Discovery happens entirely through Bazaar. No custom registry, no on-chain catalog.

## Revised architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Spectopus Server               │
│                (Express + x402)             │
│                                             │
│  GET /skills/search ──── free, in Bazaar    │
│  GET /skills/:id ─────── $0.01, in Bazaar   │
│  POST /skills/generate ─ $0.10, in Bazaar   │
│                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │        Generation Pipeline          │    │
│  │  Research → Generate → Validate     │    │
│  │  (code)     (LLM)      (LLM+script) │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                             │
│  ┌──────────┐                               │
│  │  SQLite  │  ← stores generated skills    │
│  └──────────┘                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ↑ discovered via
    x402 Bazaar (automatic)
```

This is lean enough to build in 2 days and has zero redundant infrastructure. Shall I start?

content.text:
Got it. Much cleaner. Here's what I understand now:

## Architecture

```
POST /skills/generate   → $0.10 x402 → research+generate+validate → store to S3 → return SKILL.md
GET  /skills/:id        → $0.01 x402 → fetch from S3 → return SKILL.md

Both registered in Bazaar.
Storage: S3-compatible (e.g. Cloudflare R2, MinIO, etc.)
Skill ID: random hash (content hash or UUID)
```

## Generation pipeline

```
1. Research (agent with tools)
   Tools: fetch ABI, fetch verified source, detect ERC patterns
   LLM: analyze tool output, identify key functions, gotchas, patterns

2. Generate (LLM)
   Input: research analysis
   Output: SKILL.md following Agent Skills spec

3. Validate (LLM + script)
   Script: skills-ref validate
   LLM: cross-check code examples against ABI signatures
   Loop back to step 2 if issues found
```

## Discovery (out of scope, but demo-ready)

Ship a script/skill that uses `withBazaar` from `@x402/extensions` to search for Spectopus skills. This becomes part of the onboarding — "install this skill to let your agent discover and buy skills from Spectopus."

---

No database, no custom catalog, no free endpoints, no on-chain registry. Just an API server, S3, and Bazaar.

Ready to start building?

input.content:
# Spectopus — Project Requirements

> AI-powered agent skill generator for smart contracts.
> Built for The Synthesis hackathon (Theme 2: Agents that trust).

## Problem

AI agents need to interact with smart contracts but lack reliable, structured knowledge about how to do so. Currently they either:

- Waste context tokens parsing raw ABIs and docs with unreliable results
- Hallucinate function signatures, causing failed transactions and wasted gas
- Ask the human for help, defeating the purpose of autonomy
- Give up entirely

Agents could generate skills themselves, but it takes time, domain knowledge, and specialized tools. Spectopus provides a cheaper, faster, higher-quality alternative — and builds a growing library of reusable skills that other agents can purchase.

## Target Persona

**Primary: Autonomous AI agents** acting on behalf of developers or end-users.

- Mid-task, encounters a contract it doesn't know how to use
- Needs a working skill immediately, without human intervention
- Pays programmatically via x402 (no accounts, no API keys)
- Context window is precious — needs concise, reliable instructions

**Secondary: Developers** building agent-powered applications.

- Needs skills for multiple contracts, writing each manually takes hours
- Wants predictable quality and cost savings ($0.10/skill vs hours of work)

### Pain Points

- No reliable way to go from "contract address" to "working agent skill"
- Raw ABIs are structured but lack usage context, gotchas, and patterns
- Every failed contract call costs real gas
- No standardized discovery mechanism for agent skills

### Fears

- A bad skill causes the agent to drain the user's wallet
- Paying for a skill that doesn't actually work
- Platform lock-in (skills should be portable, open-format files)

### What Wins Them Over

- Output is open standard (Agent Skills spec) — no lock-in
- Skills are concise and context-efficient (< 500 lines, < 5000 tokens)
- Generated skills include gotchas, safety warnings, and tested code examples
- Pay-per-use via x402 — no subscriptions, no accounts

## Solution

An HTTP API server that:

1. **Generates** Agent Skills (SKILL.md) from smart contract addresses via a multi-stage AI pipeline
2. **Stores** generated skills in S3-compatible object storage
3. **Serves** skills behind x402 paywalls
4. **Registers** each generated skill on x402 Bazaar for discovery by other agents

## Architecture

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                Spectopus Server                  │
│              (Express + x402 middleware)          │
│                                                  │
│  POST /skills/generate ── $0.10 USDC via x402   │
│    → runs generation pipeline                    │
│    → stores SKILL.md in S3                       │
│    → registers new skill endpoint on Bazaar      │
│    → returns SKILL.md to caller                  │
│                                                  │
│  GET /skills/:id ───────── $0.01 USDC via x402   │
│    → fetches SKILL.md from S3                    │
│    → returns to caller                           │
│                                                  │
│  Both endpoints registered in x402 Bazaar        │
│  for discovery by any agent.                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │                        │
         ▼                        ▼
   S3-compatible              x402 Bazaar
   object storage          (discovery layer)
```

## API Endpoints

### POST /skills/generate

Generates a new Agent Skill from a smart contract address.

**Payment:** $0.10 USDC via x402 (Base Sepolia for PoC).

**Request body:**
```json
{
  "contractAddress": "0x...",
  "chainId": 84532,
  "description": "Optional brief description of what this contract does"
}
```

**Response (200):**
```json
{
  "id": "<random-hash>",
  "name": "uniswap-v3-router",
  "description": "Interact with Uniswap V3 Router: swap tokens, quote prices...",
  "skillUrl": "/skills/<random-hash>"
}
```

**Side effects:**
- Stores generated SKILL.md in S3 under the skill ID
- Dynamically registers `GET /skills/<id>` as a new x402 Bazaar resource

### GET /skills/:id

Downloads a previously generated Agent Skill.

**Payment:** $0.01 USDC via x402 (Base Sepolia for PoC).

**Response (200):** Raw SKILL.md content (`text/markdown`).

**Response (404):** Skill not found.

## Generation Pipeline

Three-stage pipeline within a single request handler:

### Stage 1: Research (AI agent with deterministic tools)

Tools available to the agent:
- **Fetch ABI** — from block explorer API (Basescan/Etherscan) by contract address + chain ID
- **Fetch verified source code** — from block explorer if available
- **Detect ERC patterns** — identify known standards (ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155, etc.) from function signatures

The LLM analyzes tool outputs to:
- Identify key user-facing functions vs internal/admin functions
- Recognize common patterns (approve+transferFrom, swap routes, etc.)
- Note potential gotchas (payable functions, reentrancy patterns, approval requirements)
- Understand the contract's purpose and typical usage flows

### Stage 2: Generate (LLM)

System prompt includes:
- Agent Skills specification (frontmatter format, directory structure, best practices)
- Smart contract skill template with recommended sections

Input: structured research output from Stage 1.

Output: complete SKILL.md following the Agent Skills spec, including:
- Correct frontmatter (name, description)
- Step-by-step instructions for key functions
- Code examples using viem (Base ecosystem standard)
- Gotchas section with contract-specific warnings
- Reference to ABI (bundled or inline)

### Stage 3: Validate (LLM + script)

- **Spec validation:** run `skills-ref validate` against generated SKILL.md (if available, otherwise validate frontmatter programmatically)
- **ABI cross-check (LLM):** verify that code examples reference real function signatures, correct parameter types, and proper return values from the ABI
- **Safety check (LLM):** ensure skill includes warnings for payable functions, approval patterns, and potential footguns

If validation fails, feed errors back to Stage 2 and retry (max 2 loops).

## Output Format

Generated skills follow the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification.md):

```
<skill-id>/
├── SKILL.md          # Instructions + metadata (< 500 lines)
└── references/
    └── abi.json      # Contract ABI (optional, for large ABIs)
```

### SKILL.md Structure

```markdown
---
name: <contract-name>
description: <what it does and when to use it>
metadata:
  contractAddress: "0x..."
  chainId: "84532"
  generator: "spectopus"
---

# <Contract Name>

## When to use this skill
...

## Setup
...

## Key functions

### <function-name>
...

## Gotchas
...
```

## Technology Stack

- **Runtime:** Node.js
- **Framework:** Express
- **Payments:** x402 (Express middleware, `@x402/express`, `@x402/evm`)
- **Discovery:** x402 Bazaar (`@x402/extensions`)
- **Storage:** S3-compatible object storage (e.g. Cloudflare R2 or MinIO)
- **LLM:** Claude (via Anthropic SDK) for generation and validation stages
- **Block explorer API:** Basescan/Etherscan API for ABI and source code fetching
- **Network:** Base Sepolia (testnet) only for PoC

## Payment Configuration

- **Network:** Base Sepolia (`eip155:84532`)
- **Token:** USDC (testnet)
- **Facilitator:** `https://x402.org/facilitator` (testnet)
- **Pricing:**
  - Skill generation: $0.10
  - Skill download: $0.01

## x402 Bazaar Integration

Both endpoints are registered in Bazaar with `discoverable: true`:

- `POST /skills/generate` — registered at server startup
- `GET /skills/:id` — registered dynamically after each successful generation

Each dynamically registered skill includes Bazaar metadata:
- Description of what the skill covers
- Target contract address and chain ID
- Input/output schema

## Demo / Onboarding

Ship a demo script (or onboarding skill) that uses `withBazaar` from `@x402/extensions` to:

1. Query x402 Bazaar for Spectopus skills
2. Browse available skills by contract/description
3. Purchase and download a skill

This demonstrates the full agent journey: discover → evaluate → pay → use.

## Hackathon Context

- **Hackathon:** The Synthesis (synthesis.md)
- **Theme:** Agents that trust — open discovery, verifiable service quality, portable agent credentials
- **Agent identity:** Spectopus, registered via ERC-8004 on Base Mainnet
- **Agent harness:** Claude Code
- **Model:** claude-sonnet-4-6
- **Participant ID:** cf5b792c0b414a84a93ebbaccd7ab392
- **Team ID:** dd3f4200c4834220b99b671793469eb4

## Out of Scope (for PoC)

- Support for non-EVM chains
- Support for HTTP APIs, local tools, or other protocol types
- Custom skill catalog / search endpoint (Bazaar handles discovery)
- On-chain skill registry or attestations
- Rating / reputation system
- Skill versioning or updates
- GUI / dashboard
- Subscription or tiered pricing
- Base Mainnet deployment (testnet only)

# Spectopus — Project Requirements

> AI-powered agent skill generator for smart contracts.
> Built for The Synthesis hackathon (Theme 2: Agents that trust).

## Problem

AI agents need to interact with smart contracts but lack reliable, structured knowledge about how to do so. Currently they either:

- Waste context tokens parsing raw ABIs and docs with unreliable results
- Hallucinate function signatures, causing failed transactions and wasted gas
- Ask the human for help, defeating the purpose of autonomy
- Give up entirely

Agents could generate skills themselves, but it takes time, domain knowledge, and specialized tools. Spectopus provides a cheaper, faster, higher-quality alternative — and builds a growing library of reusable skills that other agents can purchase.

## Target Persona

**Primary: Autonomous AI agents** acting on behalf of developers or end-users.

- Mid-task, encounters a contract it doesn't know how to use
- Needs a working skill immediately, without human intervention
- Pays programmatically via x402 (no accounts, no API keys)
- Context window is precious — needs concise, reliable instructions

**Secondary: Developers** building agent-powered applications.

- Needs skills for multiple contracts, writing each manually takes hours
- Wants predictable quality and cost savings ($0.10/skill vs hours of work)

### Pain Points

- No reliable way to go from "contract address" to "working agent skill"
- Raw ABIs are structured but lack usage context, gotchas, and patterns
- Every failed contract call costs real gas
- No standardized discovery mechanism for agent skills

### Potential Blockers

- A bad skill causes the agent to drain the user's wallet
- Paying for a skill that doesn't actually work
- Platform lock-in (skills should be portable, open-format files)

### What Wins Them Over

- Output is open standard (Agent Skills spec) — no lock-in
- Skills are concise and context-efficient (< 500 lines, < 5000 tokens)
- Generated skills include gotchas, safety warnings, and tested code examples
- Pay-per-use via x402 — no subscriptions, no accounts

## Solution

An HTTP API server that:

1. **Generates** Agent Skills (SKILL.md) from smart contract addresses via a multi-stage AI pipeline
2. **Stores** generated skills in S3-compatible object storage
3. **Serves** skills behind x402 paywalls
4. **Registers** each generated skill on x402 Bazaar for discovery by other agents

## Architecture

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                Spectopus Server                  │
│              (Express + x402 middleware)          │
│                                                  │
│  POST /skills/generate ── $0.10 USDC via x402   │
│    → runs generation pipeline                    │
│    → stores SKILL.md in S3                       │
│    → registers new skill endpoint on Bazaar      │
│    → returns SKILL.md to caller                  │
│                                                  │
│  GET /skills/:id ───────── $0.01 USDC via x402   │
│    → fetches SKILL.md from S3                    │
│    → returns to caller                           │
│                                                  │
│  Both endpoints registered in x402 Bazaar        │
│  for discovery by any agent.                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │                        │
         ▼                        ▼
   S3-compatible              x402 Bazaar
   object storage          (discovery layer)
```

## API Endpoints

### POST /skills/generate

Generates a new Agent Skill from a smart contract address.

**Payment:** $0.10 USDC via x402 (Base Sepolia for PoC).

**Request body:**
```json
{
  "contractAddress": "0x...",
  "chainId": 84532,
  "message": "Optional information about intent or anything extra that may be relevant"
}
```

**Response (200):**
```json
{
  "id": "<random-hash>",
  "url": "https://spectopus.com/skills/<random-hash>"
}
```

**Side effects:**
- Stores generated SKILL.md in S3 under the skill ID
- Dynamically registers `GET /skills/<id>` as a new x402 Bazaar resource

### GET /skills/:id

Downloads a previously generated Agent Skill.

**Payment:** $0.01 USDC via x402 (Base Sepolia for PoC).

**Response (200):** Raw SKILL.md content (`text/markdown`).

**Response (404):** Skill not found.

## Generation Pipeline

Three-stage pipeline within a single request handler:

### Stage 1: Research (AI agent with deterministic tools)

Tools available to the agent:
- **Fetch ABI** — from block explorer API (Basescan/Etherscan) by contract address + chain ID
- **Fetch verified source code** — from block explorer if available
- **Detect ERC patterns** — identify known standards (ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155, etc.) from function signatures

The LLM analyzes tool outputs to:
- Identify key user-facing functions vs internal/admin functions
- Recognize common patterns (approve+transferFrom, swap routes, etc.)
- Note potential gotchas (payable functions, reentrancy patterns, approval requirements)
- Understand the contract's purpose and typical usage flows

### Stage 2: Generate (LLM)

System prompt includes:
- Agent Skills specification (frontmatter format, directory structure, best practices)
- Smart contract skill template with recommended sections

Input: structured research output from Stage 1.

Output: complete SKILL.md following the Agent Skills spec, including:
- Correct frontmatter (name, description)
- Step-by-step instructions for key functions
- Code examples using viem (Base ecosystem standard)
- Gotchas section with contract-specific warnings
- Reference to ABI (bundled or inline)

### Stage 3: Validate (LLM + script)

- **Spec validation:** run `skills-ref validate` against generated SKILL.md (if available, otherwise validate frontmatter programmatically)
- **ABI cross-check (LLM):** verify that code examples reference real function signatures, correct parameter types, and proper return values from the ABI
- **Safety check (LLM):** ensure skill includes warnings for payable functions, approval patterns, and potential footguns

If validation fails, feed errors back to Stage 2 and retry (max 2 loops).

## Output Format

Generated SKILL.md follow the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification.md).

### SKILL.md Structure

```markdown
---
name: <contract-name>
description: <what it does and when to use it>
metadata:
  contractAddress: "0x..."
  chainId: "84532"
  generator: "spectopus"
---

# <Contract Name>

## When to use this skill
...

## Setup
...

## Key functions

### <function-name>
...

## Gotchas
...
```

## Technology Stack

- **Runtime:** Node.js
- **Framework:** Express
- **Payments:** x402 (Express middleware, `@x402/express`, `@x402/evm`)
- **Discovery:** x402 Bazaar (`@x402/extensions`)
- **Storage:** S3-compatible object storage (e.g. Cloudflare R2 or MinIO)
- **LLM:** Claude (via Anthropic SDK) for generation and validation stages
- **Block explorer API:** Basescan/Etherscan API for ABI and source code fetching
- **Network:** Base Sepolia (testnet) only for PoC

## Payment Configuration

- **Network:** Base Sepolia (`eip155:84532`)
- **Token:** USDC (testnet)
- **Facilitator:** `https://x402.org/facilitator` (testnet)
- **Pricing:**
  - Skill generation: $0.10
  - Skill download: $0.01

## x402 Bazaar Integration

Both endpoints are registered in Bazaar with `discoverable: true`:

- `POST /skills/generate` — registered at server startup
- `GET /skills/:id` — registered dynamically after each successful generation

Each dynamically registered skill includes Bazaar metadata:
- Description of what the skill covers
- Target contract address and chain ID
- Input/output schema

## Demo / Onboarding

Ship a demo script (or onboarding skill) that uses `withBazaar` from `@x402/extensions` to:

1. Query x402 Bazaar for Spectopus skills
2. Browse available skills by contract/description
3. Purchase and download a skill

This demonstrates the full agent journey: discover → evaluate → pay → use.

## Hackathon Context

- **Hackathon:** The Synthesis (synthesis.md)
- **Theme:** Agents that trust — open discovery, verifiable service quality, portable agent credentials
- **Agent identity:** Spectopus, registered via ERC-8004 on Base Mainnet
- **Agent harness:** Claude Code
- **Model:** claude-sonnet-4-6
- **Participant ID:** cf5b792c0b414a84a93ebbaccd7ab392
- **Team ID:** dd3f4200c4834220b99b671793469eb4

## Out of Scope (for PoC)

- Base Mainnet deployment (testnet only)
- Support for other EVM and non-EMV chains
- Support for HTTP APIs, local tools, or other protocol types
- Custom skill catalog / search endpoint (Bazaar handles discovery)
- On-chain skill registry or attestations
- Rating / reputation system
- Skill versioning or updates
- GUI / dashboard
- Subscription or tiered pricing
- Extra skill artifacts (resources, etc)

- [x402 Bazaar — Discovery Layer](https://docs.x402.org/extensions/bazaar.md)
- [Agent Skills Specification](https://agentskills.io/llms.txt)

## Solution

- Wants predictable quality and cost savings ($0.10/skill vs hours of work)

### Pain Points

- No reliable way to go from "contract address" to "working agent skill"
- Raw ABIs are structured but lack usage context, gotchas, and patterns
- Every failed contract call costs real gas
- No standardized discovery mechanism for agent skills

### Potential Blockers

- A bad skill causes the agent to drain the user's wallet
- Paying for a skill that doesn't actually work
- Platform lock-in (skills should be portable, open-format files)

### What Wins Them Over

- Output is open standard (Agent Skills spec) — no lock-in
- Skills are concise and context-efficient (< 500 lines, < 5000 tokens)
- Generated skills include gotchas, safety warnings, and tested code examples
- Pay-per-use via x402 — no subscriptions, no accounts

## External References

- [x402 — Open Payment Standard](https://docs.x402.org/introduction.md)
- [x402 Bazaar — Discovery Layer](https://docs.x402.org/extensions/bazaar.md)
- [Agent Skills Specification](https://agentskills.io/llms.txt)

## Solution

An HTTP API server that:

1. **Generates** Agent Skills (SKILL.md) from smart contract addresses via a multi-stage AI pipeline
2. **Stores** generated skills in S3-compatible object storage
3. **Serves** skills behind x402 paywalls
4. **Registers** each generated skill on x402 Bazaar for discovery by other agents

## Architecture

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                Spectopus Server                  │
│              (Express + x402 middleware)          │
│                                                  │
│  POST /skills/generate ── $0.10 USDC via x402   │
│    → runs generation pipeline                    │
│    → stores SKILL.md in S3                       │
│    → registers new skill endpoint on Bazaar      │
│    → returns SKILL.md to caller                  │
│                                                  │
│  GET /skills/:id ───────── $0.01 USDC via x402   │
│    → fetches SKILL.md from S3                    │
│    → returns to caller                           │
│                                                  │
│  Both endpoints registered in x402 Bazaar        │
│  for discovery by any agent.                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │                        │
         ▼                        ▼
   S3-compatible              x402 Bazaar
   object storage          (discovery layer)
```

## API Endpoints

### POST /skills/generate

Generates a new Agent Skill from a smart contract address.

**Payment:** $0.10 USDC via x402 (Base Sepolia for PoC).

**Request body:**
```json
{
  "contractAddress": "0x...",
  "chainId": 84532,
  "message": "Optional information about intent or anything extra that may be relevant"
}
```

**Response (200):**
```json
{
  "id": "<random-hash>",
  "url": "https://spectopus.com/skills/<random-hash>"
}
```

**Side effects:**
- Stores generated SKILL.md in S3 under the skill ID
- Dynamically registers `GET /skills/<id>` as a new x402 Bazaar resource

### GET /skills/:id

Downloads a previously generated Agent Skill.

**Payment:** $0.01 USDC via x402 (Base Sepolia for PoC).

**Response (200):** Raw SKILL.md content (`text/markdown`).

**Response (404):** Skill not found.

## Generation Pipeline

Three-stage pipeline within a single request handler:

### Stage 1: Research (AI agent with deterministic tools)

Tools available to the agent:
- **Fetch ABI** — from block explorer API (Basescan/Etherscan) by contract address + chain ID
- **Fetch verified source code** — from block explorer if available
- **Detect ERC patterns** — identify known standards (ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155, etc.) from function signatures

The LLM analyzes tool outputs to:
- Identify key user-facing functions vs internal/admin functions
- Recognize common patterns (approve+transferFrom, swap routes, etc.)
- Note potential gotchas (payable functions, reentrancy patterns, approval requirements)
- Understand the contract's purpose and typical usage flows

### Stage 2: Generate (LLM)

System prompt includes:
- Agent Skills specification (frontmatter format, directory structure, best practices)
- Smart contract skill template with recommended sections

Input: structured research output from Stage 1.

Output: complete SKILL.md following the Agent Skills spec, including:
- Correct frontmatter (name, description)
- Step-by-step instructions for key functions
- Code examples using viem (Base ecosystem standard)
- Gotchas section with contract-specific warnings
- Reference to ABI (bundled or inline)

### Stage 3: Validate (LLM + script)

- **Spec validation:** run `skills-ref validate` against generated SKILL.md (if available, otherwise validate frontmatter programmatically)
- **ABI cross-check (LLM):** verify that code examples reference real function signatures, correct parameter types, and proper return values from the ABI
- **Safety check (LLM):** ensure skill includes warnings for payable functions, approval patterns, and potential footguns

If validation fails, feed errors back to Stage 2 and retry (max 2 loops).

## Output Format

Generated SKILL.md follow the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification.md).

### SKILL.md Structure

```markdown
---
name: <contract-name>
description: <what it does and when to use it>
metadata:
  contractAddress: "0x..."
  chainId: "84532"
  generator: "spectopus"
---

# <Contract Name>

## When to use this skill
...

## Setup
...

## Key functions

### <function-name>
...

## Gotchas
...
```

## Technology Stack

- **Runtime:** Node.js
- **Framework:** Express
- **Payments:** x402 (Express middleware, `@x402/express`, `@x402/evm`)
- **Discovery:** x402 Bazaar (`@x402/extensions`)
- **Storage:** S3-compatible object storage (e.g. Cloudflare R2 or MinIO)
- **LLM:** Claude (via Anthropic SDK) for generation and validation stages
- **Block explorer API:** Basescan/Etherscan API for ABI and source code fetching
- **Network:** Base Sepolia (testnet) only for PoC

## Payment Configuration

- **Network:** Base Sepolia (`eip155:84532`)
- **Token:** USDC (testnet)
- **Facilitator:** `https://x402.org/facilitator` (testnet)
- **Pricing:**
  - Skill generation: $0.10
  - Skill download: $0.01

## x402 Bazaar Integration

Both endpoints are registered in Bazaar with `discoverable: true`:

- `POST /skills/generate` — registered at server startup
- `GET /skills/:id` — registered dynamically after each successful generation

Each dynamically registered skill includes Bazaar metadata:
- Description of what the skill covers
- Target contract address and chain ID
- Input/output schema

## Demo / Onboarding

Ship a demo script (or onboarding skill) that uses `withBazaar` from `@x402/extensions` to:

1. Query x402 Bazaar for Spectopus skills
2. Browse available skills by contract/description
3. Purchase and download a skill

This demonstrates the full agent journey: discover → evaluate → pay → use.

## Hackathon Context

- **Hackathon:** The Synthesis (synthesis.md)
- **Theme:** Agents that trust — open discovery, verifiable service quality, portable agent credentials
- **Agent identity:** Spectopus, registered via ERC-8004 on Base Mainnet
- **Agent harness:** Claude Code
- **Model:** claude-sonnet-4-6
- **Participant ID:** cf5b792c0b414a84a93ebbaccd7ab392
- **Team ID:** dd3f4200c4834220b99b671793469eb4

## Out of Scope (for PoC)

- Base Mainnet deployment (testnet only)
- Support for other EVM and non-EMV chains
- Support for HTTP APIs, local tools, or other protocol types
- Custom skill catalog / search endpoint (Bazaar handles discovery)
- On-chain skill registry or attestations
- Rating / reputation system
- Skill versioning or updates
- GUI / dashboard
- Subscription or tiered pricing
- Extra skill artifacts (resources, etc)

- Support for HTTP APIs, local tools, or other protocol types
- Custom skill catalog / search endpoint (Bazaar handles discovery)
